text: The robotic surgical view shows the gallbladder highlighted in green, with small adhesions marked in yellow from previous inflammation. The liver and the duodenum are also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a cautery hook to lyse adhesions around the gallbladder. The common bile duct is highlighted in green, running between the liver and small intestine, while the yellow marks the callous lymph node, indicating the area for dissection.

The surgeon begins the dissection by opening the outermost layer of the peritoneum overlying the gallbladder using a robotic hook. The hook slides in and lifts the tissue away, demonstrating the enhanced maneuverability of the robotic platform.

The surgeon carefully dissects back and forth to achieve the critical view of safety, ensuring the cystic duct and cystic artery are clearly visible entering the gallbladder without intervening structures. The common bile duct is easily identifiable in this patient.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully place plastic clips across the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring that the common bile duct and other critical structures are not occluded.

The surgeon uses a cautery device to carefully cut across the cystic artery, followed by the cystic duct, with previously placed clips visible. The gallbladder is then removed from the liver bed at an accelerated speed.
The surgeon approaches the top of the gallbladder, where the plane between the gallbladder and the liver is less distinct, likely due to inflammation from previous gallbladder attacks.

The gallbladder is completely removed, and the surgeon inspects the clips for any signs of bleeding. An assistant inserts a retrieval bag through the port in the left upper side, and the gallbladder is placed inside the bag for removal.
